title: Childhood Obesity vs Precocious Puberty — Height and Weight Acceleration in a 3-Year-Old Boy
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat) — resident training module (Master Vinayak Mishra, Patient #31)

relatedChapters.title: Disorders of Growth and Development: Clinical Perspectives
Disorders of Growth and Development: Diagnosis and Treatment
CPP with Coincidental Primary Hypothyroidism (Van Wyk-Grumbach trap)

items.title: How to read Master Vinayak's presentation — height and weight acceleration at age 3
items.content: Master Vinayak Mishra, 3 years old, presents with height and weight acceleration for age — both centiles rising on the growth chart. At age 3, the primary diagnostic dilemma is differentiating true precocious puberty (pathological early sex-steroid exposure with secondary sexual characteristics and/or gonadotropin activation) from obesity-driven hypergrowth (increased linear growth velocity from metabolic and hormonal changes of excess adiposity, without true pubertal activation). The management plan correctly targets three first-line investigations: bone age (left wrist/hand X-ray), thyroid function (TSH and free T4), and an overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test to assess serum cortisol. The fellow must integrate the physical examination — especially Tanner staging and testicular volume — with these three tests before declaring the child 'just obese' or 'truly precocious.'
keyPoints: 3y boy: height AND weight acceleration — dual centile rise
Core fork: true precocious puberty vs obesity-driven hypergrowth
First-line work-up: bone age, thyroid function, overnight dex suppression

items.title: Why precocious puberty in a 3-year-old boy is always pathological
items.content: Precocious puberty is defined as the appearance of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in girls and before age 9 in boys. A 3-year-old boy with any true pubertal sign — testicular enlargement (>4 mL), penile growth beyond infancy, or pubic hair — has precocious puberty by definition and requires full endocrine evaluation regardless of BMI. This is distinct from obesity-related growth acceleration, where the child may be tall and heavy but lacks gonadotropin-driven pubertal changes and retains prepubertal testes (<4 mL). The age of 3 years makes the threshold question stark: either pubertal signs are present (pathology) or they are absent (favour obesity-related hypergrowth, but bone age and endocrine screening still apply).
keyPoints: Boys: precocious puberty = secondary sexual characteristics before age 9
At 3 years, any true pubertal sign is pathological — full work-up mandatory
Obesity hypergrowth: tall/heavy without testicular enlargement or pubertal staging

items.title: How obesity drives height and weight acceleration without true puberty
items.content: Childhood obesity increases linear growth velocity through several mechanisms: hyperinsulinism augments GH secretion and IGF-1 bioavailability at the growth plate; excess adipose tissue aromatises adrenal androgens to oestrogens, which can mildly advance bone age; and nutrition excess supports somatic growth. The result is a child who tracks high on both height and weight centiles — often with BMI SDS disproportionately elevated relative to height SDS. Crucially, the testes remain prepubertal (<4 mL), the penis is infantile in size, and pubic hair is absent or limited to benign premature adrenarche (which can occur independently around age 6–8 but may appear earlier in obese children). Growth velocity is increased but driven by nutrition and insulin, not gonadotropin-dependent testicular testosterone production.
keyPoints: Obesity → hyperinsulinism → increased GH/IGF-1 action at growth plate
Peripheral aromatisation mildly advances bone age without true puberty
Prepubertal testes (<4 mL), no penile/pubic pubertal changes = obesity pattern

items.title: Why testicular volume is the single most important physical examination finding
items.content: The orchidometer resolves the obesity-vs-puberty fork at the bedside. Central precocious puberty (CPP) is LH/FSH-driven: gonadotropins stimulate Leydig and Sertoli cells, so testicular volume enlarges to >4 mL as the first sign of true puberty in boys. Obesity-related hypergrowth leaves testes prepubertal (<4 mL) even when the child is tall and heavy. Penile enlargement without testicular enlargement suggests adrenal androgen exposure (premature adrenarche, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, exogenous androgens) rather than true gonadotropin-driven puberty — the 'pubic hair without gonadarche' pattern. For Master Vinayak, palpate testicular volume, measure stretched penile length, document Tanner staging for pubic hair and genitalia, and plot height velocity and BMI SDS — before any blood draw.
keyPoints: Testes >4 mL → think true CPP (LH/FSH-driven)
Testes <4 mL with tall stature → obesity hypergrowth or peripheral androgen source
Penile enlargement without testicular enlargement → adrenal/peripheral, not CPP

items.title: Why bone age is the anchor investigation in this work-up
items.content: A left hand-and-wrist X-ray for bone age (Greulich-Pyle or Tanner-Whitehouse) is the most informative single investigation in the obesity-vs-precocity dilemma. Sex steroids — whether from true puberty, peripheral sources, or aromatisation in obesity — advance skeletal maturation. The pattern matters: (1) Bone age markedly advanced (>2 SD above chronological age) with pubertal signs strongly suggests true sex-steroid exposure requiring full puberty work-up. (2) Bone age mildly advanced, concordant with height age, in a child with prepubertal testes favours obesity-related maturation. (3) Bone age delayed relative to chronological age suggests hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency, or chronic disease — not obesity hypergrowth. Bone age also feeds height prediction: advanced BA in true precocity threatens premature epiphyseal fusion and reduced final height.
keyPoints: BA >> CA + pubertal signs → true sex-steroid exposure; full puberty work-up
BA mildly advanced, prepubertal testes → obesity-related pattern
BA delayed → hypothyroidism/GHD/chronic disease — not simple obesity

items.title: How to interpret bone age when obesity and precocity both seem possible
items.content: In obese prepubertal children, bone age is often mildly advanced (6–12 months ahead) from peripheral aromatisation of adrenal androgens — this alone does not diagnose precocious puberty. The red flags for true precocity are: bone age advanced >2 years beyond chronological age; advancement out of proportion to height age; and concurrent pubertal examination findings (testicular enlargement, penile growth, progressive pubic hair). If Master Vinayak's bone age reads 5 years at chronological age 3 with prepubertal testes, the advancement may reflect obesity; if bone age reads 6–7 years with testicular volumes of 6 mL, true CPP is likely. Always interpret bone age alongside the genital examination, not in isolation.
keyPoints: Obesity alone: BA mildly advanced (~6–12 months), prepubertal testes
Red flags: BA >2 years ahead, disproportionate to height age, pubertal exam findings
Never interpret BA without concurrent genital/Tanner examination

items.title: Why thyroid function is essential in any child with growth acceleration
items.content: Thyroid disorders alter growth and must be excluded. Primary hypothyroidism typically causes growth failure and delayed bone age — but severe, long-standing hypothyroidism can cause precocious puberty in girls (Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome: ovarian cysts, vaginal bleeding, delayed bone age, suppressed gonadotropins). In boys this is rare. Hyperthyroidism, conversely, increases growth velocity and bone age advancement with weight loss or poor weight gain — not the pattern here if weight is also accelerating. Subclinical hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, normal T4) may coexist with obesity and metabolic syndrome. For this 3-year-old with height and weight acceleration, TSH and free T4 screen for unsuspected thyroid disease that could mimic or coexist with the growth pattern and must be corrected before interpreting other hormonal results.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism: usually growth failure + delayed BA; VWG precocity rare in boys
Hyperthyroidism: increased growth velocity — usually with weight loss
TSH + free T4 mandatory in any growth disorder work-up

items.title: Why an overnight dexamethasone suppression test is included in this work-up
items.content: The overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (1 mg/m² dexamethasone at 23:00, cortisol measured at 08:00) screens for Cushing syndrome — pathological hypercortisolism. In children, endogenous Cushing classically presents as weight gain with growth failure (short and fat), not height acceleration — making it an unlikely cause of Master Vinayak's dual height-weight rise. However, mild hypercortisolism, exogenous glucocorticoid exposure (inhaled, topical, oral), or an adrenal adenoma in a toddler can present atypically, and obesity itself can complicate cortisol interpretation. The test is included to exclude hypercortisolism as a contributor to the weight gain and to catch the trap of attributing a Cushingoid appearance entirely to simple obesity. Suppressed post-dexamethasone cortisol (<1.8 µg/dL or assay-specific cutoff) excludes Cushing; failure to suppress demands further testing.
keyPoints: Classic paediatric Cushing: weight gain + growth failure (short and fat) — unlikely here
Overnight low-dose dex suppression screens for hypercortisolism despite atypical presentation
Exogenous steroids (inhaled/topical) — take drug history; can suppress axis

items.title: How to interpret dexamethasone suppression in a young obese child
items.content: Obesity can mildly elevate baseline cortisol and blunts the suppressibility threshold, so interpret the overnight dex test in clinical context. A post-dexamethasone cortisol <1.8 µg/dL (or below the laboratory's paediatric cutoff) effectively excludes Cushing syndrome. Failure to suppress — especially with a Cushingoid habitus (moon facies, buffalo hump, purple striae, proximal weakness) — triggers a full Cushing work-up: repeat low-dose dex test, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, and ACTH level to separate ACTH-dependent (pituitary, ectopic) from ACTH-independent (adrenal) causes. In a 3-year-old, adrenal causes predominate if endogenous Cushing is confirmed (see pe-cr-004). A normal suppression test in an obese child with height acceleration supports the obesity hypergrowth diagnosis.
keyPoints: Post-dex cortisol below lab cutoff excludes Cushing
Failure to suppress + Cushingoid features → full Cushing work-up (ACTH, urine/salivary cortisol)
In toddlers, adrenal causes predominate if endogenous Cushing confirmed

items.title: How central precocious puberty differs from peripheral precocity in boys
items.content: If true precocious puberty is confirmed on examination, classify the mechanism. Central precocious puberty (CPP): early activation of the HPG axis — LH and FSH rise, testicular enlargement occurs first, pubertal progression is sequential. Causes include hypothalamic hamartoma (classic in young boys), CNS tumours, prior CNS injury, and idiopathic CPP (less common in very young boys — organic cause must be excluded with MRI). Peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) precocity: sex steroids arise independently of the HPG axis — LH/FSH are suppressed; causes include adrenal tumours, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, hCG-secreting tumours (hepatoblastoma, germ-cell), McCune-Albright syndrome, and exogenous sex steroids. The testicular volume and gonadotropin pattern separate these — but the first step in this case is determining whether any true precocity exists at all.
keyPoints: CPP: LH/FSH elevated, testicular enlargement first, sequential progression
Peripheral: sex steroids high, gonadotropins suppressed, testes may stay small
Very young boys with CPP — exclude hypothalamic hamartoma/CNS lesion with MRI

items.title: Why premature adrenarche is a common mimic in obese young boys
items.content: Premature adrenarche — early appearance of pubic hair, axillary hair, or body odour from increased adrenal androgen secretion — is common in obese children and is not true precocious puberty. It occurs when adrenal androgen production rises before the normal age of adrenarche (~6–8 years), driven in obesity by hyperinsulinism stimulating the adrenal zona reticularis. The child may have pubic hair (Tanner P2–P3) but no testicular enlargement, no penile growth, and prepubertal gonadotropins. Bone age may be mildly advanced. This is a benign variant if isolated and non-progressive — but virilisation (clitoromegaly in girls, rapid phallic growth in boys), marked bone age advancement (>2 SD), or elevated 17-OHP should prompt evaluation for congenital adrenal hyperplasia or adrenal tumour.
keyPoints: Premature adrenarche: pubic hair/body odour WITHOUT testicular enlargement
Common in obesity — hyperinsulinism drives early adrenal androgen rise
Virilisation or BA >2 SD → exclude CAH and adrenal tumour

items.title: How to use growth velocity and BMI SDS to support the diagnosis
items.content: Plot height and weight on age- and sex-appropriate centile charts and calculate growth velocity over at least 6–12 months. Obesity hypergrowth shows height velocity at or above the 90th percentile for age with parallel or disproportionate weight gain (BMI SDS often >+2). True precocious puberty also increases growth velocity (the 'growth spurt' from sex steroids and GH) but adds pubertal examination findings. Calculate BMI SDS and weight-for-height — if weight is rising faster than height, simple obesity is likely; if both are accelerating with pubertal signs, precocity is the driver. Target height (mid-parental height) contextualises whether the child's height is familial or pathologically accelerated. Document the rate of centile crossing — rapid crossing over 6 months is more concerning than gradual drift.
keyPoints: Plot height velocity over ≥6 months; BMI SDS and weight-for-height
Obesity: weight often rising faster than height; precocity: both accelerate with pubertal signs
Mid-parental height contextualises familial vs pathological tall stature

items.title: What to add if bone age is markedly advanced or pubertal signs are present
items.content: If the initial trio (bone age, thyroid, dex suppression) suggests true precocity — markedly advanced bone age, testicular enlargement, or progressive pubertal signs — extend the work-up: basal LH, FSH, testosterone (early morning in boys), adrenal androgens (DHEAS, androstenedione, 17-OHP); GnRH stimulation test if basal LH is borderline (LH >0.3–0.5 IU/L on ultrasensitive assay may confirm activation without stimulation); and MRI brain with pituitary protocol for any boy with confirmed CPP under age 6 (hypothalamic hamartoma peak incidence). If peripheral precocity is suspected (high testosterone, suppressed gonadotropins, small testes), add abdominal ultrasound (adrenal, liver, gonads) and beta-hCG. Do not jump to MRI or GnRH stimulation until the examination and bone age confirm the need.
keyPoints: Advanced BA + pubertal signs → LH/FSH/testosterone, 17-OHP/DHEAS, consider GnRH test
CPP in boys <6 years → MRI brain (hypothalamic hamartoma)
Peripheral precocity pattern → abdominal US, beta-hCG

items.title: How management forks once the diagnosis is clarified
items.content: If obesity hypergrowth is confirmed (prepubertal testes, mildly advanced bone age, normal thyroid, suppressed cortisol on dex test, no pubertal progression): management is lifestyle — dietary modification, physical activity, family-based behavioural intervention; monitor growth and bone age annually; treat comorbidities (MASLD, dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance). No GnRH agonist is indicated. If true CPP is confirmed: treat with GnRH agonist (leuprolide, triptorelin) to halt pubertal progression and protect final height; investigate CNS cause with MRI; manage coincident hypothyroidism if present. If Cushing is detected: treat the hypercortisolism (surgical for adrenal/pituitary tumour, taper exogenous steroids). The three-test initial screen prevents overtreatment of obese children with GnRH agonists — a common and costly error.
keyPoints: Obesity hypergrowth → lifestyle intervention; no GnRH agonist
True CPP → GnRH agonist + MRI; protect final height
Initial screen prevents GnRH agonist overtreatment of obese prepubertal children

items.title: How the stepwise work-up sequences for Master Vinayak
items.content: Step 1 — Bedside: Tanner staging, testicular volume (orchidometer), stretched penile length, pubic hair; plot height velocity and BMI SDS. Step 2 — First-line labs/imaging (as ordered): bone age X-ray; TSH and free T4; overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test. Step 3 — Interpret the fork: prepubertal testes + mildly advanced BA + normal thyroid + suppressed cortisol = obesity hypergrowth → lifestyle management. Markedly advanced BA and/or pubertal signs = true precocity → Step 4: LH/FSH/testosterone, 17-OHP, GnRH stimulation if needed, MRI brain if CPP confirmed in a 3-year-old boy. Step 5 — Treat by diagnosis; reassess growth and bone age at 6–12 months. The teaching punchline: the orchidometer and bone age together resolve the dilemma before expensive hormonal dynamic testing.
keyPoints: Exam (testes, Tanner) → bone age + TSH + dex suppression → interpret fork
Obesity pattern → lifestyle; precocity pattern → hormonal work-up + MRI
Orchidometer + bone age resolve the dilemma early

items.title: Obesity vs precocious puberty — fellow work-up checklist
items.content: ☐ Plot height, weight, BMI SDS, and growth velocity (≥6 months). ☐ Tanner staging: genitalia (G), pubic hair (P). ☐ Testicular volume (orchidometer) — document mL bilaterally. ☐ Stretched penile length. ☐ Bone age X-ray (left wrist/hand). ☐ TSH and free T4. ☐ Overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression → morning cortisol. ☐ If pubertal signs or BA >> CA: LH, FSH, testosterone, DHEAS, 17-OHP. ☐ If CPP confirmed in boy <6y: MRI brain with pituitary protocol. ☐ Medication history: exogenous steroids (inhaled, topical, oral). ☐ Do not start GnRH agonist until true CPP is confirmed.
keyPoints: Exam + bone age + thyroid + dex suppression = mandatory first pass
GnRH agonist only after confirmed CPP — not for obesity alone
MRI if CPP in boy <6 years

items.question: Why is testicular volume more informative than height centile alone in this 3-year-old boy?
items.answer: Because height acceleration occurs in both obesity hypergrowth and true precocious puberty, so height centile alone cannot separate them. Testicular enlargement (>4 mL) is the hallmark of gonadotropin-driven puberty in boys — it reflects LH/FSH stimulation of the testes. Obesity increases linear growth without enlarging the testes. Therefore orchidometry distinguishes the two mechanisms at the bedside before any hormonal testing.
keyPoints: Height acceleration is non-specific — occurs in both obesity and precocity
Testes >4 mL = gonadotropin-driven puberty
Prepubertal testes with tall stature favours obesity hypergrowth

items.question: How should a fellow use bone age, thyroid function, and dexamethasone suppression together to differentiate obesity from precocious puberty?
items.answer: First, perform genital examination and orchidometry. Then: (1) Bone age — mildly advanced (≤1 year) with prepubertal testes favours obesity; markedly advanced (>2 years) with pubertal signs favours true precocity. (2) Thyroid — hypothyroidism with delayed bone age excludes simple obesity hypergrowth; hyperthyroidism may accelerate growth; subclinical hypothyroidism may coexist. (3) Dex suppression — excludes Cushing as a cause of weight gain; classic Cushing causes growth failure, so normal suppression in a growing obese child supports obesity. Integrate all three with the examination: prepubertal testes + mildly advanced BA + normal thyroid + suppressed cortisol = obesity hypergrowth; pubertal testes + markedly advanced BA = proceed to LH/FSH and MRI.
keyPoints: BA mildly advanced + prepubertal testes → obesity
BA markedly advanced + pubertal signs → precocity work-up
Normal thyroid + suppressed post-dex cortisol support obesity pattern

items.stem: A 3-year-old boy has height and weight acceleration. On examination, testes are 2 mL bilaterally with no pubic hair and infantile penis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Obesity-driven hypergrowth without true precocious puberty
Central precocious puberty requiring urgent GnRH agonist
Testicular tumour causing isosexual precocity
Congenital adrenal hyperplasia with marked virilisation
items.explanation: Prepubertal testes (2 mL), no pubic hair, and infantile penis exclude true gonadotropin-driven puberty. Height and weight acceleration with prepubertal gonads favours obesity-related hypergrowth. CPP requires testicular enlargement (>4 mL). CAH with virilisation would show penile enlargement and pubic hair.

items.stem: Bone age in this boy is 4.5 years at chronological age 3 years. Testes remain 2 mL. What is the best interpretation?
options: Mild bone age advancement consistent with obesity-related aromatisation — not diagnostic of true precocious puberty
Definite central precocious puberty requiring immediate GnRH agonist
Delayed bone age suggesting hypothyroidism
Bone age is irrelevant when height is accelerating
items.explanation: Mild bone age advancement (~1.5 years) with prepubertal testes is typical of obesity-related peripheral aromatisation, not true CPP. Markedly advanced bone age with testicular enlargement would suggest precocity. Bone age is delayed in hypothyroidism, not mildly advanced.

items.stem: If the same boy had testicular volume of 6 mL bilaterally and bone age of 6 years at chronological age 3, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Basal LH/FSH and testosterone; MRI brain with pituitary protocol if CPP confirmed
Reassurance and lifestyle advice only
Overnight dexamethasone suppression test as the sole investigation
Immediate bilateral orchidectomy
items.explanation: Testicular enlargement (6 mL) with bone age 3 years ahead at age 3 indicates true precocious puberty. Basal gonadotropins and testosterone classify central vs peripheral; MRI brain is mandatory in young boys with CPP to exclude hypothalamic hamartoma. Lifestyle alone is insufficient; orchidectomy is never appropriate.

items.stem: Why is an overnight dexamethasone suppression test included in the work-up of a 3-year-old with height and weight acceleration?
options: To exclude Cushing syndrome as a cause of weight gain, even though classic paediatric Cushing typically causes growth failure
Because Cushing syndrome is the commonest cause of tall stature in toddlers
To diagnose growth hormone deficiency
Because dexamethasone treats obesity-related hypergrowth
items.explanation: The test screens for hypercortisolism contributing to weight gain. Classic paediatric Cushing presents as short stature with weight gain, but mild or exogenous hypercortisolism should be excluded. Cushing is not a cause of tall stature; dex suppression does not diagnose GHD or treat obesity.

items.stem: TSH is 8 mIU/L with normal free T4. Bone age is delayed at 2 years (chronological age 3). Testes are prepubertal. What does this pattern suggest?
options: Subclinical hypothyroidism with delayed bone age — not obesity hypergrowth; treat with levothyroxine
Normal variant requiring no action
Central precocious puberty
Hyperthyroidism causing growth acceleration
items.explanation: Elevated TSH with delayed bone age indicates hypothyroidism — which causes growth failure and delayed skeletal maturation, not obesity hypergrowth. Levothyroxine is indicated. This pattern excludes CPP (which advances bone age) and hyperthyroidism (which suppresses TSH).

items.stem: A 3-year-old obese boy has pubic hair (Tanner P2) but testes of 2 mL and no penile enlargement. Bone age is mildly advanced. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Premature adrenarche — early adrenal androgen rise without true gonadarche
Central precocious puberty with gonadotropin activation
McCune-Albright syndrome requiring immediate surgery
Normal physiological puberty for age
items.explanation: Pubic hair without testicular enlargement or penile growth is premature adrenarche (adrenal androgen source), common in obesity. CPP requires testicular enlargement first. McCune-Albright would show café-au-lait spots and more marked bone age advancement. Physiological puberty does not occur at age 3.

items.statement: A 3-year-old boy with height acceleration but prepubertal testes (<4 mL) and no secondary sexual characteristics can be managed as simple obesity without any laboratory or radiological investigation.
items.explanation: False. Even when examination favours obesity hypergrowth, bone age, thyroid function, and dexamethasone suppression are indicated to exclude precocious puberty, hypothyroidism, and Cushing. Investigation prevents misclassification and overtreatment.

items.statement: Childhood obesity can increase linear growth velocity through hyperinsulinism and increased IGF-1 bioavailability without true gonadotropin-driven puberty.
items.explanation: True. Obesity increases GH secretion and IGF-1 action at the growth plate via hyperinsulinism, and peripheral aromatisation may mildly advance bone age — all without testicular enlargement or gonadotropin activation.

items.statement: Classic endogenous Cushing syndrome in children typically presents with height acceleration and weight gain.
items.explanation: False. Classic paediatric Cushing causes growth failure (short stature) with weight gain — the 'short and fat' pattern. Height acceleration with weight gain favours obesity or precocious puberty, not classic Cushing.

items.assertion: Testicular volume should be measured before ordering gonadotropin levels in a boy with height acceleration.
items.reason: Prepubertal testes (<4 mL) indicate that height acceleration is not from gonadotropin-driven puberty, making basal LH/FSH unnecessary as a first step.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Orchidometry is the bedside fork; prepubertal testes exclude CPP and defer the need for gonadotropin testing until bone age and other screens are done. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: GnRH agonist therapy should be started in every obese boy with height acceleration.
items.reason: GnRH agonists halt gonadotropin-driven pubertal progression and protect final adult height in true central precocious puberty.
items.explanation: The assertion is false (GnRH agonists are only for confirmed CPP, not obesity hypergrowth) but the reason is true (GnRH agonists work in true CPP). Starting GnRH agonists in obese prepubertal boys is a common overtreatment error. Hence option 3.

items.assertion: Mild bone age advancement alone confirms precocious puberty in an obese child.
items.reason: Obesity-related peripheral aromatisation of adrenal androgens can mildly advance bone age without true gonadotropin-driven puberty.
items.explanation: The assertion is false (mild BA advancement alone is non-specific) but the reason is true (obesity can mildly advance BA via aromatisation). BA must be interpreted with genital examination. Hence option 3.

items.question: List the three first-line investigations ordered for Master Vinayak and state what each contributes to the obesity-vs-precocity fork.
items.answer: (1) Bone age (left wrist/hand X-ray) — distinguishes mild obesity-related advancement from marked precocious advancement; feeds height prediction. (2) Thyroid function (TSH, free T4) — excludes hypothyroidism (delayed BA, growth failure) and hyperthyroidism. (3) Overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test — excludes Cushing syndrome as a cause of weight gain; classic Cushing causes growth failure but must be screened.

items.question: Name three physical examination findings that favour obesity hypergrowth over true precocious puberty in a 3-year-old boy.
items.answer: (1) Prepubertal testicular volume (<4 mL bilaterally). (2) Infantile penis without true penile enlargement. (3) Absence of progressive pubic hair or pubic hair only (premature adrenarche) without concurrent testicular enlargement — the 'pubic hair without gonadarche' pattern.

items.question: If true central precocious puberty is confirmed in this 3-year-old boy, name two mandatory next steps.
items.answer: (1) MRI brain with pituitary protocol to exclude hypothalamic hamartoma and other CNS lesions — mandatory in young boys with CPP. (2) Initiate GnRH agonist therapy (e.g. leuprolide depot) to halt pubertal progression and protect final adult height, after excluding CNS pathology requiring neurosurgical intervention.
